chunks.text: The committee was very interested in progress on this particular issue. We had a briefing quite some time ago and understood that there were some difficulties, but because of Ministry of Health and MPI cross involvement there. If you could fire away and tell us where we're at, and I'm sure there'll be plenty of questions.

I can. I should first preface by saying I prepared some notes that start a bit earlier than that, but I saw your interim report, and you obviously know quite a lot about it already. If I start a reasonable way through the- Yep... story, I may not have too much to say, but- It's all right.

We may be finished quite quickly. But just very briefly, as I'm sure you all know, hemp is a form of cannabis that's got very low THC, and THC is the main psychoactive substance. And so it's wholly unsuitable as a recreational drug. But it is nevertheless regulated under the misuse of drugs legislation, which is why we ended up with this review topic. A

year ago when, I think Tim came and talked to you, the situation was that we had reasonably comprehensive regulation around a licensing regime that involved record keeping and monitoring and reporting obligations and so on. And when we were briefed about this, our take on it was that that was a little bit over the top for the risk involved, which is why we got interested in it as a ministry.

I should say, just very briefly, I'm not sure how across members are of the functions of the Ministry of Regulation. We've got quite a broad range of functions. But one of the areas that we work on is reviews of current legislation. And that kind of comes in two flavors.

One flavor is that we do what we call sector reviews, which are mid-sized reviews of systems of regulation, like the regulation around the importation of agricultural and horticultural products, for example, or solar panel installation regulation and so on.

And then there's a much smaller limb which is quite reactive, where we get tips from the public or from other sources, and we look at much smaller areas of regulation, and this was in that latter category. So we'd had intel that this industry was possibly out regulated, and that's how I got interested in it.

And we got interested because it looked as if the industry was underperforming compared to what you might expect. There were projections for it to grow quite significantly. Some projections were around the tens of millions of dollars. Some were around much higher than that, although they were possibly a bit optimistic.

And the industry was sitting in the very low millions of dollars of revenue. And so it looked like something was holding it back. And it was possible that it was this regime. And the regime, we felt, was possibly holding it back in a few ways. First of all, although the compliance costs weren't particularly high, they were still a factor.

Secondly, the need to get a license delays how quickly you can shift crops and we had heard anecdotally that there was potential for a crop failure and for that crop to be quickly replaced with a hemp crop because it grows quite quickly. But the time that it took to get a license was standing in the way of that, and so land would often stand unused in that situation instead of being used for hemp. So that was a second thing.

Thirdly, we had heard that the restriction on the types of cultivars that could be used was potentially affecting the competitiveness of the industry. And then there was another set of issues that I'll come to later that unfortunately we couldn't get into.

But I'll come to those a bit later. So we got interested in it because it looked like there could be a regulatory solution here, and that's kind of our bread and butter, to try and find things that are being held up by regulation that we might be able to, at very low risk, improve. You commented on the fact that we'd had a bit of trouble with, or there'd been a bit of trouble with the Ministry of Health and Ministry of Primary Industries.

That actually wasn't really our experience. The Ministry of Health was very supportive of the review, because there were compliance costs at their end factoring to administer the regime, which they didn't think was worthwhile. And so they were pretty supportive the whole way through and provided a lot of the technical expertise that we didn't have in-house around the issues.

MPI did have some concerns, but the scope of the review was narrowed so that those concerns weren't-Weren't very much triggered, and so they also ended up not being detractors. So our relationship with both those agencies during the review was actually pretty good, and they were pretty supportive, certainly in terms of giving technical expertise where we needed it.

Just to move to the concerns that MPI did have. So their most major concern was around the use of hemp in animal feed, and we didn't include that in the scope of the review, and that is not being changed. It is under, as I understand it, constant ongoing discussion between the industry and MPI to see whether there's some kind of solution. Because it would be fantastic for the industry if they could use hemp for animal feed because it would take something that they have to dispose of at quite a lot of cost and turn it into something

they could sell instead. But there are real sensitivities in some of our export markets about any hemp ending up in the food chain. And so, the conclusion is that we would put really big export industries at risk if we went down that track.

And MPI was very nervous about that, and with pretty good reason, I think. And I've been on calls between them and the industry where the industry does appear to accept that that is a pretty major risk that has to be carefully navigated. So that put a review of the ACVM legislation out of scope of our review.

It did remain a small concern for MPI because they were worried that, if there was a proliferation of people growing hemp, that some people might just feed it to animals anyway. And the way that we dealt with that was that anybody who wants to grow industrial hemp, going forward, I'm probably getting a little bit ahead of myself because I'll talk about where the review got to, but just to finish the point, going forward, will have to notify MPI before they plant, which will give MPI the opportunity to reach out to them and stress the

importance of the hemp not getting close to animals that might end up in the export food chain. Oh, sorry, did you have a question? But that notification is not the same as them requiring to get a license directly from MPI. Just a notification. Just a notification. Okay. That's right.

Yep. So where we got to on our recommendations in the end was that we recommended that the license regime be removed entirely really. So that got rid of the record-keeping obligations and reporting obligations and so on, and meant that the people could be more nimble.

There are a few restrictions that remain in place. So you still need a license to import or export. There are restrictions on what you can do with the plant. So this is really the deregulation's really around the stalks, and the seeds. So if people want to use the leaves and flowers, they really need to sell them into a licensed medicinal cannabis scheme.

Because that's where the cannabinoids are. There was an old restriction under the old regime, separate from the license, which said that industrial hemp growers could only sell a limited number of plants and seeds to medicinal cannabis schemes. We saw no reason for that, so we removed that as well.

So they at least can sell unlimited amounts into those medicinal cannabis schemes after the change. There is also still the restriction that I talked about around animal feed. And we introduced a new requirement, which was to replace the licensing, which was that before anybody planted, they needed to notify both the police and MPI.

The MPI bit was so that MPI could do its outreach. The police bit was because there was a concern that criminal interests might see this as an opportunity to piggyback on a growing industry and potentially use it as a money-laundering operation that could also disguise an illicit cannabis growing operation.

That was kind of the concern. And so the requirement to notify police before you plant was intended as a deterrent for that largely. And if that did start happening as a source of intel for the police. So that was a slightly new thing. There was previously a notification requirement, but it was a bit loose because it was after planting, and within a kind of reasonable period of time.

Whereas we replaced that with the requirement that the notification had to be before planting and annually updated, so that if police found any plants on any property and there wasn't a notification in place that was less than a year old, then they could deem that to be illicit cannabis.

So that was kind of our bright-line way of trying to deter the criminal elements from getting involved. The recommendations of the review were accepted, umComplete by Cabinet and the new secondary legislation to bring it into effect has been promulgated, but we're within the 28 days before it becomes effective. So the new regime is not yet effective but will be very soon.

So that's really all I have by way of update. And I probably can't string it out too much more. Does anybody on the committee have any questions? So it can be affected by an order in council without any legislative change. But what about the Misuse of Drugs Act stuff?

So the Misuse of Drugs Acts contemplates that you can permit certain uses of the cannabis plant through regulations, and so this can all be done through regulations. Mm. And so the regulations are permissive of industrial hemp being grown, distributed, sold, basically used in any way apart from the restrictions that I talked about before, which largely relate to the flowers and leaves- Mm... and use for animal feed, which is in a different set of legislation.

So I'm still not clear about the Misuse of Drugs Act bit of it. So the Misuse of Drugs Act, prevents you from doing a whole lot of things with cannabis because it's a class C drug. Mm. But it allows regulations to be passed- Right. Okay... that allow you to do things. Right. And the regulations allow this. So we already had regula- since 2006, we've had regulations that have allowed some use of industrial hemp.

Right. It's just that they came with a whole lot of conditions attached, and we were kind of stripping away those conditions. And one final one. Okay. How does the industry feel about it? The industry. So in the early consultation, the industry was a little bit split about it. But we ended up mainly talking to a few representatives who have become very positive about it over time. The industry's view on it is a little bit split depending on the type of business they have. So exporters saw some benefit in a licensing regime

because it gave a government stamp of approval to the product that they were exporting, and so that they had some kind of attachment to it. But we didn't really hear that loudly all the way through. So even the people who had that interest kind of said, "Oh, no, we do support the changes," but they kind of mulled over whether there might be anything they could do to keep something like that. In the end, we convinced them that the import-export licensing requirements that stayed in place, and they have to stay in place because of our international obligations-

Oh... were going to be sufficient to give that stamp of approval. Mm-hmm. And our take on it was that people got pretty confident about that. The second concern from the industry, is around cross-pollination risk. So if there was a proliferation of low-quality hemp being planted, there is a risk that that could affect the quality of their crops.

And that is a bit of an ongoing concern for the industry. It's a concern across all of horticulture. Mm. And it's generally managed by the industry, and there are kind of systems that they do to manage where they each plant. And the industry's working on moving into those systems.

And MPI is supporting that to some degree. I think the industry would like a little bit more, but our take on it from the outside is that it's kind of a reasonable balance at the moment. Mm. Would it- MPI's got a lot to worry about, a lot of other things. Mm-hmm. Just as a suck on that, it would fall in within the current seed certification sort of parameters anyway, wouldn't it?
So the seed certification is gone, so you'll be able to plant- No, I mean the general seed certification, not just for- Oh, you mean the food part? No, there's the seed certification where you have

seed that's grown and harvested and processed for either pasture use or use to be or for export or whatever it is. MPI have a seed certification scheme that does look at those things. Yes. Purity and germination and the cultivars and things like that.

So my understanding is that it's kind of a system thing with a facilitator being set up, but they're not that hands-on about it. I think it is an industry-funded and run thing across horticulture. That's my understanding. So they pointed the industry to people who are already doing that for other horticultural industries to try and help out and connect them up with people who might be able to help them along those sorts of lines.

That's right. Yeah. Ingrid. Yeah. I've got some growers in my electorate who have been pushing for this. Oh, okay. And I'm curious to know, my understanding it's to do with competitiveness with Australia. Have you seen modeling or have they given you numbers of what the difference would make now with the economic impact, say a year hence or three or five years hence?

So we haven't seen numbers. There is some analysis that was done about how the industry could grow in New Zealand. It's because it's much bigger in Australia.I have the numbers here somewhere, but I don't know how credible they are. It was pretty hard to get into it because it seemed- Can I-... quite advocacy driven.

Sure. But the- We did get a series of numbers- Right... a year or so ago from hemp industry themselves. They gave us quite a bit of analysis. Okay. I think the, so the MPI analysis suggested it could grow in New Zealand to about 30 million by 2030.

And at the moment it's sitting around about a million, I understand. Yeah. So it's got very low. But there were other projections that we saw that suggested it could grow as large as a billion dollars by 2030. That didn't seem credible to us. And- The economic blip we did really just took those types of projections from MPI at face value.

We didn't do our own modeling or anything like that. There were also claims that this was an existential question for parts of the sector. Is that an overstatement or is that your understanding that without this regulatory change, that some of the businesses would've had to- I would say-... live on online clients... I would say that's credible because-... the industry certainly was really floundering in terms of numbers. Yeah.

Cool. Yeah. I don't think this is the- And I see-... the golden bullet. Yeah, sure. But I think that they've got other challenges. But this gives them a better chance than they had, I think. Mm-hmm. Mark. Mr. Chair, thank you so much. My question sort of goes back to what was forementioned, in terms of the industry itself obviously took issue with the Misuse of Drugs Act and how it was policed.

Do you think where we are today and where the industry's ultimately wanting to go, that it's still too prohibitive in terms of police notification, growing crops, and the timelines that are afforded to do that, that it is still under this ridiculous burden of THC levels being at a certain threshold, yes or no, and therefore they can grow it, yes or no. Do you think the industry and the overall regulations are fit for purpose, or do they need further fine-tuning?

I think that they-- So I don't think there's a big burden at all with the notification. The notification is very easy. It's just an email that somebody sends. So there's no real burden there. I think the industry, both the hemp industry and probably more the medicinal cannabis industry, suffers from the perception that it's all part of a kind of illicit drug family.

And my understanding is that there are certainly elements of the police that have that attitude as well. But we heard no evidence of any kind of heavy-handed treatment of the industry or anything like that. It was more almost a branding issue for them. So at one point, I got told off for calling it marijuana, which is apparently something that really does make it sound like it's illicit.

So there are certainly sensitivities, but I don't think they're a regulatory problem. I think that the main regulatory barrier is really the, well, there are some regulatory barriers in the medicinal cannabis area- Mm... which I think could be looked at, and in fact, the Ministry of Health are looking at those.

But in hemp, the main regulatory barrier is, I think, the animal feed regulatory barrier. But it's not an overreach. I'd like to think there was some way that it could be modified in some way to give them some relief. But there's certainly a legitimate concern there.

It's not, in my view, a regulatory overreach. So supplementary on that, if I may, Mr. Chair. Yeah. So I think we all agree that the animal feed thing, very, very nuanced, and if we appreciate that foreign markets set the threshold for this stuff, then you're sort of dancing on the head of a pin to try and get it across the line there.

My concern was always when we started this whole process, that the industry wanted to grow and the Misuse of Drugs Act was prohibitive. You're saying that it's pretty much not, and it's more a perception thing. Correct? That's right. I think it was, but it's not-- Well, it wasn't prohibitive, but I think it was a difficulty. But I think this reform has removed that barrier pretty substantially.

Thank you so much. Right. Are there any other further questions? Excellent. Thank you very much, Adam. That's all right. Good to get an update so we know where we are. And hopefully the industry can grow a bit. I guess we'll know over the next few years if there are any other issues that they might rise, raise with us.

Yeah. So I'm still talking to them to see if there's still a line of communication open. Actually, I did hear one quite cool anecdote the other day, which is that one use for hemp that I had never thought of is that it can be used apparently to extract contaminants from soil. Mm. And yeah. And they're exploring that. I linked them up with Ministry for the Environment a couple of days ago to explore with aThat it might be able to be used with our own contaminated sites in New Zealand.

So that's the kind of thing that probably would've been too hard before the licensing- Mm... regime got removed, but that's kind of the way that we understand- Do you mean the byproduct, or do you mean the actual planting? That the planting it can purify the soil? Doesn't purify the soil. It's more about, it's an efficient way of extracting it. So the roots go down quite- Yeah... deep and-... and they get there quite fast, and they suck up some toxins in the soil, and then you harvest them.

And so you essentially pull the toxins out with the plants, and then, you burn them at a high temperature and filter out the toxins and, you're left with a kind of byproduct that can go back into the ground. So- Mm... it doesn't destroy the toxins, but it's an efficient way of extracting them from the soil.
Yeah. Very good. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thanks for your time.

Well, welcome to the-... Primary Production Select Committee. We've got 50 minutes, so, time's yours, fire away. I guess quite keen to hear your views on, obviously, the HASNO and ACVM stuff that we've seen documentation through on. Mr. Chair, Select Committee members, value and appreciate your time today and ongoing interest in this topic.

Look, three things we thought would be useful to cover off today. One, you requested a progress update since we last caught up with committee back in November 2024, around the Ministry for Regulation review and the pack that we sent through. Mm. Secondly, would be useful to talk about legislative changes. Fantastic to see the ACVM and the HASNO Amendment bills, pass first reading last week.

If interested, we could share some high-level initial member insights, noting that the bills will need really careful consideration, and we look forward to coming back to Select Committee. And then thirdly, just to chat to you beyond legislative change, where are we trying to move some solutions forward- Thanks... to make progress in this area that don't require legislative change.

So look, let's start with just emphasizing from the front that basically the views we represent here today is feedback from our members and not individuals. With me also here is Emma Waller from an AFANZ board perspective to speak to where the board's thinking is at on this. So look, let's start with since we last caught up in November, we had the Ministry for Regulation review come out. So the report came out in February.

It largely confirmed what industry had been signaling for some time. Cabinet have backed a really positive roadmap for change, and industry strongly support that. For us, we would like to say that we recognize and acknowledge the significant activity that's been underway- Mm... with regulators, so a lot of activity going on, on the ground, and it's the applicant experience that we're representing here. So what we're looking at around is the outcomes in terms of an easier process, seeing that red tape removed, and ultimately, are we getting the new products that

farmers, growers, and vets need. So what we have done in November of 2025 and February of 2026 was we went out on the ground to applicants, and we did pulse surveys. So those surveys are publicly available, and the results really speak for themselves where we're at with those. So we are hearing from applicants that they're not seeing a change in the complexity of the approval process.

So for 75% of members back in February, we did a deep dive of R&D companies, so that's both domestic New Zealand and also overseas global registrants. And ultimately, 75% of them were saying that compared to other markets, New Zealand is a less attractive place to do business.

So there's, as of May, we do note that there is some positive announcements around some of the newer product types and the new label approvals happening, but for our members, they are looking for a steady stream of them rather than a slow trickle. So ultimately, what they are looking for in terms of outcomes is an actual process that's having less red tape, and it's looking like it is getting easier.

They're seeing a stream of the new product and the new label uses come through, and that industry feedback has been incorporated into some of the operational solutions that have been put on the table to try to support. So, ultimately, we'll do another pulse member survey again soon, and we do appreciate that some of this messaging can be at times hard to hear, but ultimately, it reflects the experience that our members are having on the ground. Members of this Select Committee have also heard directly from R&D companies recently as to where

their applications are at currently, and we are supporting applicants with case examples into ministers currently as well, to give you some context. So look, the other update is what have been the industry impacts for our members since we last caught up. So we've had things like Bayer have exited R&D in Hastings after 64 years. Back in 2018, they were getting world-first registrations in this country right here.

We're hearing about a decline in the demand for critical laboratory capability to ensure that R&D data can be used offshore, so internationally used.Companion animal. We're dealing with a case study at the moment where a very simple label difference between Australia and New Zealand, 240% more to the manufacturer to bring it to New Zealand.

So again, you've heard many of these case examples individually as well as you've been out and traveled through with members. So for us, the real opportunity here is around how do we actually see that progress in terms of those three things that these companies are looking for. And for many of them, they're asking the question now since we've met, is it's no longer do we register here, it's do we supply at all?

So that's actually critical, and that's across the board, both in terms of chemistry, biologicals, the organic stuff, as well as the synthetic. So that's the choice we've got in front of us now as a country, but it's the real opportunity with the legislation as well. So really high level, if I could share some perspectives on the legislation.

Again, we'd like to acknowledge Minister Seymour, Hoggard, and Griggs for progressing the bills, and also the work of the officials and the regulators to get it where it is at. Cabinet's intent has been really clear on this, a proportionate, timely, transparent, and efficient regulatory approvals process.

So our feedback today is preliminary. There have been a heap of learnings out of the RMA and the medicines reforms as well. So today what we're providing is very high-level member feedback. We really look forward to coming to select committee on this, and we will put a really thorough submission through.

So look, industry's practical and lived experience is going to be important here too also. Our analysis looked at three really clear things to date. Do these bills deliver on Cabinet's intent? Did they listen and incorporate industry and community feedback from the initial round of proposals?

And really importantly, do they set robust principles and time frames that's actually going to enable transparency, consistency, and also that accountability? So I think if we could summarize overall, the early analysis from members is there's still a bit of a way to go in terms of are these bills going to deliver to Cabinet's intent. If we look at HSNO, there is some positive stuff in there around data protection.

But ultimately, there's also a levy in there where there's some important detail missing around principle setting, and industry are strongly against a levy unless outcomes can be demonstrated for the investment to date. Everyone supports a properly funded regulator, but ultimately, it's that pace around outcomes for investment.

For ACVM, there's been much discussed about recognizing trusted overseas regulators. A runway appears to be there, but will it deliver in practice is yet to be seen. There is already some red flags popping up around decision-making powers, criteria, the level of thresholds required, and the off-ramp.

So we will come back with more detail on that as we work through it carefully. But for Botox, one of the things that has really stood out is around time frames. And why do I talk about that, and why does that matter? When we listened to the bills being read in the first reading, we heard the concerns around consistent, equal, fair assessment for all products.

Our members support that, but that starts with sunlight, and that actually starts with transparent time frames from when an applicant actually puts the application in to when it's actually decided. So members of this committee have heard from global R&D companies case examples of where new, innovative, more modern chemistry has sat effectively for years, technically within statute, but effectively it's not making progress through the queue. So that's an opportunity there.

So is loosening those time frames up actually going to enable you to identify, measure, and fix if there is any unequal treatment happening there? So time frames are something that will be a theme when we come back and speak to you. Really quickly and finally, one of the other thoughts that has popped up across members as they've looked and listened across the bills, the precautionary principle.

So this one, this was born and originated out of the EU, okay. But in the last three decades, it has had significant refinement around how it's interpreted. There's been elements of definition ambiguity, neutrality, and other elements that they are refining that as they go.

The question we're left with is has New Zealand had that conversation yet? So it's not saying the question we want to leave select committee with is not is the precautionary principle wrong as it stands. What we're asking is a more modest and probably a more important question, did it deserve the right to a public consultation and actually to have a debate and the opportunity to update it given what's happening in the EU?

With that, I think the final comments we do have is we remain really committed to back this reform. We absolutely acknowledge the efforts and the activities of the regulators, and we are working hard around operational updates separate to the legislation that we think offer opportunity to streamline and to help manage low risks in a safer way to actually take resource off regulators. But with me today, I've had Emma Waller, who's one of our board members.

She also works closely across the Australia-New Zealand system. So I thought I'd pass to Emma to share some perspectives. So the AFANZ board is strongly behind AFANZ as our wider sector stakeholders such as Business New Zealand, Horticulture New Zealand, and NZVA, who have all signaled strong supportIn their manifestos, a proportionate, transparent, and efficient, and certain approval regulatory process is critical for all companies that want to innovate in New Zealand.

Certainty really matters. For many companies, it's become impossible to make supply chain decisions and plan for seasonal markets given the uncertainty of where the pipeline products are in the regulatory system and when they'll come through. From a personal perspective, I work across both Australian and New Zealand regulators, so I've seen firsthand the differences in our systems. In Australia, you can submit for a novel active ingredient and reasonably confidently expect to get approval in two years or less. In New Zealand,

we have a completely different experience where your applications for the same novel active ingredient will go into the regulator's pipeline, or queue, and sit there for five years, sometimes more. So it's very disheartening from a New Zealand perspective to see the same active ingredients get to the market at least three years earlier in Australia.

It would be really good to see an action plan on the memorandum of understanding, which was signed in November between our two regulators, and see what that will deliver and when. So what does success look like? These delays impact all products, be it synthetic, organic, or new biologicals.

What our members want to see is, like Liz said, a steady stream of approvals coming through, not the slow trickle that we've been experiencing for the last five or so years. A better application process and processing times through streamlined and efficient processes with no unnecessary duplication and red tape, which we're currently faced with.

We want to see red tape easing, with the opportunity to adopt solutions, such as self-assessable changes, containment approvals, sorry, group standards. These are solutions which were proposed in the review, which don't need legislative change, so they can be quick and easy ones.

The sector backs reform, but what matters most now is the throughput. It's no longer just a regulatory issue, it's an investment decision being made offshore. When 75% of our member companies say New Zealand is becoming less attractive for innovation, the risk isn't delay, but the risk is New Zealand missing out.

Thank you. We've got a couple of questions. Yes, Suz. Thank you very much. Liz, you talked about concern regarding, in terms of HASNO, you talked about concerns regarding principle setting. I just wondered what you meant by that. Precautionary. Huh? Precautionary principle.

Oh, okay. And also to clarify, we note that they're putting in a new levy, so given the learnings we've had around wider regulation, there'll be some constructive thoughts we'll have around how do you make sure that you have some really strong principles setting around that, and it's specific to the levy as well.

Oh, I see. Yeah. I think the question we had around precautionary principle was more why was it not within the scope of actually having some public consultation, given it's been updated as well in other jurisdictions as well. And so did we miss an opportunity, I guess, was the question. But the principle setting is around some thoughts we'll be bringing around that relative to the levy. Yeah.

Okay. Awesome. Yeah. Thank you. Steve. And just for clarification, you're not against the idea of a risk tier framework, it's just about transparency and logic- Precaution... around where things fit in those tiers- This is the-... so that there's an appropriate level of application of precaution to things that are greater risk, but there's a potential to process things more quickly that are of lower risk.

This is- And is there a lack of clarity on where things fit when they turn up and apply for release, where products sit into the process? If we look to what the Ministry for Regulation review found, it found that we do have a really good risk assessment system.

So that's important, and that actually matters. We're all supportive of that. I think the question was, it identified that at times it's not getting that balance and enabling actually the access to the new innovation. Mm-hmm. Now, why does that matter? Because most of the stuff that we're actually trying to bring in is more modern or is replacing older chemistry. So in terms of an appropriate risk and safeguards, that's in everybody's interest, and I think the good news is the report identified that we've got that.

But it's about how do we achieve the right risk balance. That's really the question. So it's actually if we put the safeguards to one side, what we're hearing from members on the ground is that at the moment it's that pace around can they have certainty and proportionality of the pipeline when they put a new active ingredient in to actually be approved.

So that's where time frames come in, because certainty really matters, and it's about how do you strike that balance. Can I just supplement? Yeah. So when a product application comes in, does it immediately go into a queue to be assessed linearly depending on which came in first, a first in first served? Or is there some sort of preliminary tiering of the risk at the outset so that- A new product gets set, because of its characteristics or because of overseas approvals, it can be placed in a lower risk tier or a medium

risk tier or a higher risk tier based on what the characteristics, inherent characteristics are of that product. Is that how it works? Yeah. So typically it is going in on a first, it's literally put in and it's kind of in the queue with everything else. I mean, Emma- Right. But how do they do it more efficiently overseas is, I guess, what I'm trying to understand. Yeah. So this is about are there, it's a resource focus on what are the risks that matter most.

So at an operational level, for example, some of the things we are trying to bring in at the moment is overseas there are lower risk changes. Things like where there isn't a unique to New Zealand trade or environmental risk. Is there opportunities then for applicants to self-assess lower risk changes? Emma, as someone who puts applications in all the time, do you want to comment on that in terms of where you see the opportunities around assessment of risk and where they're focusing?

Yeah, there's definitely opportunities to triage that queue. Right. In your example of lower risk applications, there are applications that go in where they've been assessed by overseas regulators, and they could be triaged into another category, for sure. And utilizing an assessment that's been done by a trusted regulator is definitely an opportunity that should be explored.

I'm surprised that that's not happened. Right, we've come to time. Thank you very much, Liz and Emma. I expect that we'll see you again through the submission process. So thank you very much for your time today. Appreciate it. Thank you. Thanks. Thank you.
